#leekumkee #crawfishseason #oystersauce #ShakeUpYourCrawfish #50bestrestaurants

If you’ve been cooking with just one or even two types of soy sauce, you’re missing out on the full potential of this varied ingredient! Not all soy sauces are the same — and using the right one really does make a difference in the final dish. Each type has its own flavor profile, color, and purpose, and once you understand the distinctions, you’ll start reaching for the right bottle instinctively. 

We’ve partnered with Lee Kum Kee @leekumkeeusa, a heritage brand founded in 1888 (with their invention of another staple Chinese ingredient—oyster sauce), to break down the six types of Chinese soy sauce that we think every home cook should have on their radar.

Here’s what sets each one apart, how to use it (including suggested recipes!) and why each one deserves a spot in your pantry.

🥢 Comment “soy sauce” and we’ll send over our full guide with what recipes you can make with each type of soy sauce! ❤️

After climbing 28 places in the list this year, Albi in Washington DC wins the Highest Climber Award 2026, sponsored by Lee Kum Kee, and is also The Best Restaurant in Northeast USA 2026. 

Opened in 2020 in DC’s Navy Yard Neighbourhood, Albi has fast become a local favourite for its modern take on Palestinian cuisine. Here, chef-owner Michael Rafidi is deeply inspired by his family’s roots in Ramallah, as well as memories of cooking in his grandparents’ kitchen. Much like its namesake – Albi means ‘my heart’ in Arabic – Rafidi’s menu represents the powerful connection between family, tradition and food. 

The chef’s choice five-course tasting menu explores Palestinian cuisine through an exploratory approach, with seasonal mid-Atlantic produce at the core. A prime example of Rafidi’s creative, narrative-driven approach is his Maryland blue crab, served with sweetcorn, serrano oil and a wood-fired potato pita with foraged mushrooms and hummus. Through reimagining family recipes and reconnecting to his roots, Rafidi has created one of DC’s most creative and hospitable restaurants.

Lamdre in Beijing wins the Highest Climber Award 2026, sponsored by Lee Kum Kee.

In Beijing’s vibrant Sanlitun district, Lamdre is a pioneering plant-based restaurant owned by Zhao Jia and led by chef Dai Jun. The duo shares a vision that transcends mere vegetarianism, promoting a way of life that prioritises health, sustainability and a deep respect for nature. 

Upon entering, guests are greeted by a harmonious blend of natural light and minimalist design, featuring a pitched roof skylight that illuminates the space. The serene ambience of the main dining room is complemented by handpicked ceramics and art, offering a holistic and calming retreat from the bustling streets.

Here, sustainability is paramount, with the team spotlighting ingredients that are organic, natural and biodynamic, forging connections with local farmers and markets to ensure the freshest produce. Chef Jun also frequently embarks on research trips to discover lesser-known ingredients that reflect China’s biodiversity.

The menu at Lamdre changes with the seasons, showcasing the diversity of ingredients sourced from the rich soils and culinary traditions of the region. Signature offerings include tofu made from Heilongjiang soybeans steeped in a matsutake and wakame broth; and ginkgo nuts with peach gum paired with Yunnan mushrooms in a Guizhou-style sour soup.

The one dish Vicky Cheng makes every Spring Festival. 
 
Celebrate Spring Festival with Vicky Cheng, chef and owner of Wing (No.11 on The World’s 50 Best Restaurants) and Vea (No.53 on Asia’s 50 Best Restaurants), as he reflects on his Shanghainese heritage by recreating his favourite seasonal childhood dish – Shanghai-style pork-and-shrimp wontons.
 
“Growing up, we always celebrated Spring Festival with my grandmother’s wontons. I made plenty of mistakes (and mess) learning how to make them as a child, but her reassuring presence always made cooking a joy. 
 
Everything was instinctive with her: a spoonful of this, a pinch of that, so recreating these wontons meant piecing together memories through phone calls with my mother and grandmother.
 
I don’t serve them yet in my restaurant, but I still make them at home and hope my daughters will grow up with the same memories of those flavours from childhood.”
 
Discover more renowned chefs’ favourite Spring Festival dishes via the link in bio.

Growing up, Crispy Noodles with Seafood was more than dinner, it was my mom’s way of showing love. The dish that defined my childhood and connects me to my family — I now have the chance to pass it on to future generations.

I’m preserving this memory through the Forever Flavors Project with @leekumkeeglobal and UNESCO. Have your food story officially archived too. Submit via the link in Lee Kum Kee Global’s bio.

Crispy Noodles with Seafood 
Serves 4

Ingredients

Crispy Noodles 300g dried egg noodles Neutral oil, for frying Pinch of salt

Seafood & Vegetables 1 lb shrimp, peeled 150g squid, cleaned and scored 1 bunch broccolini 1 cup carrots, bias cut 1 cup shiitake mushrooms ½ tsp salt ½ tsp sugar White pepper, to taste

Oyster Sauce Gravy 2 tbsp neutral oil 1 tbsp garlic, minced 3 cups chicken stock 2 tbsp premium oyster sauce 1½ tbsp light soy sauce 2 tsp fish sauce 2 tsp sugar 1¼ tsp sesame oil White pepper, to taste

Slurry  2 tbsp cornstarch 1½ tbsp potato starch ¼ cup cold water

Instructions

1. Boil noodles in salted water until 70–80% cooked. Drain, rinse cold, and dry very well. Air-dry 20–30 minutes, form loose nests, then fry at 375°F (190°C) until puffed, golden, and crisp. Drain on a rack and season lightly with salt.

2. Season shrimp and squid with salt, sugar, and white pepper. Set aside 15 minutes.

3. Blanch carrots, broccolini, then mushrooms in salted boiling water until just tender. Shock in ice bath, drain, and reserve.

4. In the same pot, turn off the heat. Add shrimp and poach 30 seconds, then add squid and cook another 30–40 seconds. Shock in ice bath, drain, and reserve.

5. Heat oil over medium-high. Sauté garlic until fragrant. Add stock, oyster sauce, soy sauce, fish sauce, sugar, sesame oil, and white pepper. Bring to a gentle simmer.

6. Stir slurry well and stream into the sauce while whisking. Let simmer 8–12 seconds until glossy and very thick. Remove from heat.

7. Fold seafood and vegetables into the gravy and serve over crispy noodles immediately.

“Flavour is the language we all speak.”

For @awongsw1 , it’s the only language that matters – the one everyone understands. He leads the first two-Michelin-starred Chinese restaurant outside Asia, yet he can’t read or write Chinese. But taste has become his bridge to heritage, family, and every table in between. 

In his hands, a bottle of Premium Oyster Sauce links three generations at his family table – and maps his journey of self-discovery after his father’s passing. His story might change how you see Chinese cuisine, and maybe even that familiar bottle sitting in your own kitchen. Discover his story now! 

#leekumkee #foodmemories #premiumoystersauce #BehindTheBest

The first chef outside Asia to earn two Michelin stars for Chinese cuisine… never learned Chinese properly.  @awongsw1 found another way. A language that lasts.  From his grandfather’s search for ingredients in the 70s to his father’s restaurant, Andrew inherited a journey of self-discovery. 

As a Londoner navigating his heritage, he found his answer in flavour. A universal language that lets him stay true to both heritage and himself, connecting people across generations and borders. “”This is my language. One that people can share no matter where they’re from.”” For 138 years, Lee Kum Kee has carried the same belief:. That flavour is a language that lasts.  Follow us , and watch the full episode tomorrow. 

#leekumkee #foodmemories #premiumoystersauce #BehindTheBest

PRAWN AND SCALLOP SIU MAI 🥟✨ This is a dish I make when I miss home and want to feel close to my family. It’s simple, comforting, and full of memories - the kind of food that carries more than just flavour.

If there’s a dish that carries a story for you, I’d love you to share it. Submit your food memory to Lee Kum Kee and UNESCO’s Forever Flavors Project’s official archive - the link is in @leekumkeeglobal’s bio.
 
20 - 24 Siu Mai 
 
- 400g raw prawns, peeled and deveined
- 100g fresh scallops
- 2 – 3 dried shiitake mushrooms, rehydrated and finely chopped
- 1 – 2 tbsp garlic chives, finely chopped
- 1 tsp finely chopped ginger
- 1 ½ tbsp Lee Kum Kee Premium Oyster Sauce
- 1 tsp Lee Kum Kee Premium Soy Sauce
- ½ tsp Lee Kum Kee Sesame Oil
- Dash of white pepper
- 2 tbsp tapioca starch
- Wonton wrappers, to wrap
- Tobiko (fish roe), to serve

Pulse half of the prawns in a food processor until a paste forms. Finely chop the remaining prawns by hand and set aside to give the filling some texture.

Soak the dried mushrooms in hot water for 15–20 minutes until fully rehydrated. Squeeze out any excess liquid, then finely chop.

Chop the garlic chives and mix with the sesame oil. Finely chop the ginger. In a bowl, combine the prawns, mushrooms, chives and ginger with the oyster sauce, soy sauce, white pepper and tapioca starch. Mix well until evenly combined.
 To form the siu mai, hold a wonton wrapper in your palm and spoon a heaped tablespoon of filling into the centre. Gently shape into a classic open-top siu mai, placing a scallop into the filling on top. Press lightly so it holds its shape, keeping the top loose and natural.

Arrange the siu mai in a lined steamer, leaving a little space between each one. Steam over gently boiling water for 8–10 minutes, or until just cooked through and fragrant.

Remove from the heat, top with fish roe and serve immediately.

Surprise your taste buds like @dimsimlim with this Pearl of the East cocktail, created by award-winning mixologist @ggnchan using Lee Kum Kee Premium Oyster Sauce. Full recipe below!

Ingredients:
- Lee Kum Kee Premium Oyster Sauce 2g
- Tequila 20g
- Kombu infused vodka 15g
- Filtered sea water 35g
- Fresh grapefruit juice 65g
- Rich syrup 5g
- Lemon juice 5g
- Green pepper hot sauce 4g
- Sake 35g
- Mineral water 25g
- Milk 20ml
- Oyster leaf, garnish

Directions:
- Combine all ingredients.
- Mix with 20ml of milk.
- Strain with coffee filter.
- Pour the batch into a mixing glass and stir with ice.
- Strain into glass. Garnish with an oyster leaf. Serve.

Chef @rgborago’s Chilean Abalone with Luche Seaweed using signature Lee Kum Kee sauces is truly “loco”! We love when cultures come together to create delicious, innovative dishes. Full recipe below!

Ingredients:

For the Vegetable Base & Topping:
- Lee Kum Kee Teriyaki Sauce 20ml
- Lee Kum Kee Chili Garlic Sauce 15g
- Mallow Leaves 4 pcs (one per serving)
- Hydrated and drained luche seaweed 4 sheets (or other fresh seaweed)

For the Loco:
- Chilean abalone “Loco” 4pcs
- Water 2L
- Coarse salt 20g
- Bay leaf 1pc
- ½ Onion, sliced

For the Mayo:
- 1 Fresh whole egg
- Mild vegetable oil 200ml 
- Fresh lemon juice 10ml 
- Salt 2g

Directions:
- Wash abalone thoroughly and cook in a pressure cooker with water, coarse salt, bay leaf, and onion for 50–60 min until tender. Let cool in the broth, then slice into thick rounds or medallions.
- Meanwhile, make the mayo. In a tall mixing cup, combine egg, lemon juice, and salt. Add oil and emulsify until thick mayonnaise forms. Keep refrigerated.
- Wash and dry the mallow leaves. Roast briefly on a hot pan or grill until tender and lightly charred.
- Toss the abalone slices with Lee Kum Kee Teriyaki Sauce and Lee Kum Kee Chili Garlic Sauce. Sauté for 1 min to glaze.
- Place one mallow leaf on each plate. Add a spoonful of mayo in the center, then top with glazed abalone. Cover with a sheet of hydrated luche and finish with a few extra drops of Lee Kum Kee Chili Garlic Sauce.

After climbing an impressive 19 spots, Cosme in Lima has earned the Highest Climber Award 2025, sponsored by Lee Kum Kee, as part of this year’s edition of Latin America’s 50 Best Restaurants.

After honing his skills in top kitchens around the world, James Berckemeyer returned to Peru to open Cosme in 2015, a relaxed restaurant celebrating the flavours and aromas of Peruvian home cooking. Located in San Isidro, the restaurant combines a casual, laid-back dining style with creativity in the kitchen.

Berckemeyer’s menu offers a choice of a nine-course tasting journey or a la carte highlights such as grilled Angus sweetbreads with corn and yellow chili, corn tacos with BBQ ribs, and traditional cau cau with prawns and scallops. 

The space reflects the chef’s playful spirit and passion for sustainability, featuring a ceiling installation of 5,000 recycled bottles and a wall adorned with 13,345 bottle caps, marrying unique design with Beckermeyer’s refined Peruvian comfort food.

李錦記家族捐款支援大埔火災救援工作

李錦記家族及李錦記集團一直心繫香港，對於早前大埔火災受災人士所面對的重大損失深感悲痛。為此，李文達與蔡美靈慈善基金宣佈，捐出三千萬港元予大埔宏福苑援助基金，以支援火災救援工作，並為受影響的居民提供援助，協助他們渡過難關。

我們的醬料業務紮根於大埔，近四十年來與大埔社區共同成長。大埔不僅是我們的工作場所，更是我們成長、奮鬥及生活的家園。眾多同事、家人、朋友、供應商及合作夥伴均與大埔社區緊密相連，這場悲劇讓我們倍感痛心。

我們將繼續與社會各界保持緊密合作，為受災市民提供援助。我們衷心向受影響人士致以最深切慰問，並感激每一位救援人員的無私付出。

Experience a taste of Tanière3 in Quebec City.

The restaurant delivers a multi-sensory experience that unfolds in three acts. The journey begins at a hidden entrance on la rue Saint-Pierre, accessed via a personalised code sent to diners on booking. After a dimly lit descent, guests arrive in the Bar Vault, where they are greeted with a welcome cocktail and a series of canapés in a room showcasing the ecosystem and microclimate behind Nicol’s culinary ethos. Diners either move to the Main Dining Room for a classical and intimate dining experience for up to six people, or the Chef’s Counter for an interactive experience alongside nine other guests beneath a dramatic tree root installation.

The final stop is the Dessert Vault: a bright, birch tree-filled room where desserts, mignardises and infusions are served in a surreal forest-like setting.

Tanière3 offers a dining experience that challenges conventional expectations of fine dining, especially for those who are unfamiliar with the warmth and playfulness of Quebecois hospitality.

Learn more about why Tanière3 has received the Art of Hospitality Award 2025, sponsored by Lee Kum Kee, as part of North America’s 50 Best Restaurants, via the link in bio.

#NorthAmericas50Best #50BestRestaurants @leekumkeeglobal @leekumkeecanada @taniere3

BREAKING: Ahead of the first-ever North America’s 50 Best Restaurants, Tanière3, an extraordinary dining destination in Quebec City, wins the Art of Hospitality Award 2025, sponsored by Lee Kum Kee!

Led by restaurant director and co-owner Roxan Bourdelais and chef François-Emmanuel Nicol, Tanière3 offers a unique, multi-sensory dining experience set inside historic stone vaults in the heart of Old Quebec.

Their approach is a bold, modern take on Quebecois cuisine, inspired by the wild, natural ingredients of the province. Every ingredient used on the boreal-inspired menu, described by Bourdelais as “modern, wild Quebec,” is sourced locally, whether grown, raised or foraged. It’s a masterclass in terroir-driven cuisine, where every bite is unquestionably Quebecois.

“It’s an immersive experience through culinary traditions,” says Bourdelais. “It’s not just about food, but the decor, which is unique in 400-year-old vaults.” 

Keep watching for a taste of this multi-sensory experience and to find out why Tanière3 is the deserving winner of the Art of Hospitality Award 2025, sponsored by Lee Kum Kee.

Ikoyi in London, is the winner of the Highest Climber Award 2025, sponsored by Lee Kum Kee! 

Jeremy Chan and Iré Hassan-Odukale launched Ikoyi in 2017 with little formal training between them. Since then, it has moved from its original, somewhat cramped location to a larger space on London’s The Strand, with exquisite interiors designed by David Thulstrup. 

Chan and his kitchen team remain largely unconfined by restaurant conventions, with the gifted chef creating a culinary style that defies categorisation. The result is a singular dining experience the Canadian-Chinese-British Chan terms “a journey through bold heat”.

Having jumped an outstanding 27 places on The World’s 50 Best Restaurants list from last year, Ikoyi earns the coveted Highest Climber Award 2025, sponsored by Lee Kum Kee.

“That iconic red cap of Lee Kum Kee in Mom‘s kitchen was her warmest memory.”
At just 8 years old, Tue Nguyen immigrated to the U.S. with her family. After school, she would always linger by the stove, watching her mother craft hometown flavors with Lee Kum Kee sauces—quietly planting the seed of culinary passion in her heart. Years later, she fulfilled her dream by opening her own restaurant, DiDi.

Even her signature ”Vietnamese Shaking Beef“ relies on those same classic Lee Kum Kee sauces, completing the circle of flavor.

Does your childhood hold similar Asian flavor memories? Share them with us using the hashtag #FlavorsOfAAPI!

「小时候妈妈厨房里那抹李锦记的红，是她最温暖的记忆。」
8岁移民美国的Tue Nguyen，总在放学后守着灶台看妈妈用李锦记酱油烹制家乡味。这也在她心中悄悄埋下了烹饪的种子。多年后，她如愿开设了属于自己的餐厅DiDi。她的招牌「越式摇摇牛肉」使用的也是李锦记经典酱料，美味的轮回就此完成。

"Coming to Japan transformed my life."— Hong Kong Chef Tommy’s Tokyo Dream Journey. From working in a Chinese restaurant to becoming a Michelin-starred chef, Tommy dedicated 25 years to introducing Hong Kong cuisine into Tokyo’s culinary scene. As the head chef of Tokyo’s first Michelin-starred Hong Kong-style restaurant, he innovatively combines Japanese omakase with genuine Hong Kong flavors. Each dish reflects his commitment to preserving Hong Kong’s culinary heritage while showcasing his profound affection for his adopted homeland.

「來日本改變了我的一生。」——香港廚師Tommy的東京星夢之旅。從中餐館打工到米其林一星主廚，他用25年時間將港式料理帶進東京美食版圖。作為東京首家摘星港式餐廳的料理長，Tommy創新地將日式Omakase與港式風味完美融合，每一道菜都是他對香港味道的堅持，也是對第二故鄉最深情的告白。

We are proud to be the Official Sauce and Condiment Partner of @theworlds50best and showcased during #Asias50BestRestaurants 2025 — a celebration of culinary excellence in the region. 

At the Awards, we had the honor of presenting the Highest Climber Award to Chef Tam’s Seasons, acclaimed as the Best Restaurant in Macau. This recognition reinforces our commitment to Empowering Asian Culinary Greatness and nurturing the culinary talent of the region.

Reflecting on our time in Seoul, we were inspired by the remarkable achievements and creativity that define Asia’s vibrant culinary landscape — highlighting our products in new ways with culinary cocktails created in partnership with Wynn Macau’s Wing Lei Bar and Seoul’s Pine&Co, and wrapping up the celebrations with a delicious fusion Chinese x Korean menu featured at a Lee Kum Kee after party in collaboration with Hong Kong’s Chef May Chow (Asia’s Best Female Chef 2017) and Chef Sungjae Han of Vinology Seoul. 

The week was a gathering of culinary luminaries that showcased the passion and innovation propelling our industry forward.

Looking towards the future, we eagerly anticipate the next milestone - The World’s 50 Best Restaurants awards in Turin this June - where we will continue to celebrate culinary excellence and mastery on a global stage. Lee Kum Kee remains dedicated to delivering innovative experiences and enhancing Asian culinary culture worldwide.

Where there are people, there is Lee Kum Kee.

我們榮幸成為「50佳餐廳」官方指定醬料與調味品合作夥伴，並參與2025年亞洲50佳餐廳盛典——這場共慶亞洲美食卓越成就的巔峰盛會。 頒獎典禮上，我們很榮幸向澳門最佳餐廳譚卉頒發「最佳進步獎」。這份榮譽印證了我們閃耀亞洲美食之巔，以及培育本土烹飪人才的堅定承諾。 回首這次首爾之旅，這片美食沃土上迸發的非凡成就與創意令我們深受啟迪——我們與永利澳門「永利吧」及首爾「Pine&Co」聯手打造醬料特調，以嶄新形式演繹產品魅力；更攜手2017亞洲最佳女廚師香港主廚周思薇與首爾「Vinology」主廚Sungjae Han，在李錦記晚宴上呈現驚豔的中韓融合宴會，為此次慶典畫上美味句點。 這一周的美食界群英薈萃，充分展現了推動行業前進的激情與創新。 展望未來，我們已迫不及待迎接下一里程碑——「世界50佳餐廳」頒獎禮。屆時我們將繼續在全球舞台弘揚卓越烹飪和匠心精神。李錦記始終致力於創造革新體驗，讓亞洲飲食文化閃耀世界。 有人的地方，就有李錦記。

Experience Cantonese cuisine in the finest of settings.

Located inside the lavish Wynn Palace in Macau, Chef Tam’s Seasons serves exquisite Cantonese dishes made with the finest seasonal ingredients. The menu offers both a la carte and tasting options, including signature dishes like crispy fried chicken with golden caviar and double-boiled pork shank with conch.

The restaurant’s opulent interior features gold, jewels and emerald jade, complemented by a lush floral garden with over 5,000 roses and exotic flowers.

Chef Tam, renowned for his work at Jade Dragon in Macau, brought his expertise to Wynn in 2018, leading the restaurant to a No.36 ranking in Asia’s 50 Best Restaurants in 2019. With a gold medal from the World Champion of Chinese Cooking, he has cooked for dignitaries and royal families, and in 2025, his restaurant earned accolades as The Best Restaurant in Macau and the Highest Climber Award, sponsored by Lee Kum Kee.

At a private after party following Asia’s 50 Best Restaurants 2025 in Seoul, Chef May Chow from Hong Kong and Chef Sungjae Han of Vinology Wine Bar in Seoul presented a focused menu rooted in regional flavours with a modern edge.

Chef May Chow served Dan Dan Makguksu with buckwheat noodles dressed in a nutty, spicy sauce using Lee Kum Kee Mala Red Chilli Oil and perilla. Her Stuffed Chicken Wing was filled with freshly made cuttlefish paste, glazed with Lee Kum Kee XO Sauce and Premium Oyster Sauce. The HK Style Beef Offal Jjigae combined daikon, minari and offals in a rich broth deepened with Chiu Chow Chilli Oil.

Chef Sungjae Han’s Gungjung Tteokbokki featured LA galbi, handmade rice cakes and a soy base using Lee Kum Kee’s light and dark soy sauces. The Namul Gimpap was clean and herbaceous, with Wando seaweed and sesame sauce. Yukgaejang arrived with tender Hanwoo brisket and a fragrant broth lifted by Tingly Green Peppercorn Oil. The Vasak offered crisp kimbugak with lotus root, peanuts and a savoury dipping sauce.

The collaboration brought together two perspectives on modern Asian comfort food, served to an industry audience behind closed doors.

I had loads of fun learning from Chef Stella (@felicejeong) of @jeonghui_siktak. This lady is a multi-hyphenate who has even published a cookbook (watch me flip through it later in my Reel) who also happens to be best friends and ex-schoolmates with @chef_louishan of @naeum.sg in Singapore.
All the ingredients Chef Stella used in her cooking demonstration, were locally grown and sourced. So we had the opportunity to enjoy Hanwoo beef as well as Korean vegetables (the zucchini looked so much like avocado and tasted remarkably sweet), tofu and assorted mushrooms in the classic Korean dishes she whipped up. The clever twist though is how she used @leekumkeeglobal Premium Oyster Sauce and Chow Chow Chili Oil in them. Surprisingly, they complemented the Korean condiments beautifully. These are the creations by her:
1. Crispy Bulgogi with Oyster Sauce – Chef Stella’s version was brilliant as she pan-fried the marinated beef so the “Maillard reaction” made it even more fragrant and flavourful than the usual. It tasted great eaten Ssam-style with the fresh lettuce and perilla leaves, as well as the…
2. Oyster Sauce & Chili Oil Ssamjang – The kick of heat in this rich and spicy Korean dipping sauce was really appetising.
3. Fresh Salad with Oyster Sauce Dressing – Balancing the full-bodied flavours of the other dishes was a light salad with a tangy dressing. 
4. Silken Tofu Stew with Oyster Sauce & Chili Oil - Rounding off the meal was this bold spicy stew with softest tofu and eggs. Comfort food at its finest.
We were lucky to have paired the food with a bottle of red which @berniceliuofficial had kindly brought along. Produced by her, the silky Bordeaux blend complemented the dishes so nicely.
Dessert was unexpected but oh so good. Chef Stella cut and de-seeded semi-dried persimmon and topped each piece with #LeeKumKee chilli oil so each bite was a blend of sweet and heat. Yums!
Grateful to @mooneevv for being our amazing interpreter. We would not have been to absorb and appreciate so much of what Chef Stella was sharing today if not for her. 

#LeeKumKee #LKK #LKKat50Best
#jeonghuisiktak

Jeonghui’s Table hosted a hands-on Korean cooking class using Lee Kum Kee as its prime ingredient, introducing accessible Korean home-style dishes with a focus on flavour and ease. The session by Jeonghui Siktak highlighted recipes suitable for casual home cooking, with participants encouraged to recreate them beyond their travels.

The class opened with a silken tofu stew, served without broth but still packed with intensity. The combination of Lee Kum Kee Premium Oyster Sauce and Chow Chow Chili Oil delivered umami depth and a satisfying heat that coated the tofu without overwhelming its delicate texture. The crispy bulgogi followed, its sweet and savoury marinade cooked to a golden crust. The dish balanced caramelised edges with tender beef, making it a practical yet flavourful take on the classic.

A house-made ssamjang offered a richer and spicier profile than usual, using oyster sauce and chilli oil to add complexity. It paired well with fresh lettuce and the bulgogi, creating a satisfying wrap. The session concluded with a light salad, brought to life with a tangy dressing that echoed the session’s theme of simplicity and bold taste.

The class presented Jeonghui’s approach to everyday Korean cooking: unfussy, full of flavour, and easily replicated. Participants left not only fed but equipped.

Eclectic gastronomic institution bursting with Latin American energy.

Rafael, the eponymous restaurant of super-successful lawyer-turned-chef Rafael Osterling, opened in 2000. For more than two decades, Osterling and his team have been cherry-picking gastronomic tropes from Peru’s historic dishes—as well as touches from the Nikkei canon and left-field inspiration from the chef’s international travels—to create their own style of cuisine.

The restaurant, set in a beautiful Art Deco townhouse, is nestled in the chic Miraflores area. of Lima. The light and airy dining room offers a comfortable atmosphere, while the food is delivered as an almost incongruous-sounding cavalcade of dishes: ceviche may be followed by pizza; grilled octopus by olives and confit garlic; and sashimi by veal gizzards with truffle and sweet peas. It might sound a little unusual, but as its two-decade history will attest, it works brilliantly.

Jumping an incredible 27 places to No.19 in this year’s edition of Latin America’s 50 Best Restaurants, Rafael earns the Highest Climber Award 2024, sponsored by Lee Kum Kee

We are honoured to receive the prestigious “LEED-NC V4.0 Platinum Certification” with our “Green Soy Sauce Fermentation Project”, which is the highest level in the rating system and we are the first company in the global fermented food industry to have received this certification.#LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) certification is the most widely used green building rating system in the world and a globally recognized symbol of sustainability achievement and leadership. Our Xinhui Production Base has a comprehensive energy efficiency and environmental protection strategy to win this award. It’s equipped with a “Solar Photovoltaic Power Generation System”, “Geothermal Heat Pump System” and “Wetland Park” . In particular, the “Solar Photovoltaic Power Generation System” and “Geothermal Heat Pump System” earned full marks in the energy-saving aspect of LEED. Analysis through quantitative simulation shows that the project saves 55% more water and 50% more energy than general projects, and renewable energy constitutes 6% of its total energy consumption. To learn more, please visit: https://bit.ly/30uisHR#李錦記 除了是百年國際醬料品牌，一直也致力於綠色環保！今年，我們非常榮幸憑藉 #綠色醬油發酵工程獲得「LEED-NC V4.0」當中得分最高級別的「鉑金級認證」，成為全球發酵食品行業首家榮獲 #LEED #鉑金認證 的企業！#LEED能源與環境設計先鋒認證 是國際使用最廣泛的綠色建築評級系統，而我們的新會生產基地擁有一套全面的節能環保策略，善用 #光伏發電系統、#地源熱泵系統、#人工濕地 等環保設施，以實現可持續發展。其中光伏發電及地源熱泵這兩項重要節能措施，更在LEED節能評審中獲得滿分！通過量化模擬手段的分析，我們比常規項目節水55%，節能50%，可再生能源使用率達到項目總用能的6%！如想了解更多：https://bit.ly/2AZDOSG

【Sour and Hot Beef】

Preparation Time:  10 (mins) 
Cooking Time:  7 (mins) 
Cooking Method:  Saute Stir-fry 
Cook level:  Normal 
Serving Size:  4 (person) 
Ingredients:
Beef fillet/tenderloin 250 g
Carrot (s) 90 g
Onion(s) 1 /2pc
Chiu Chow Chili Oil 2 tbsp

Marinade:
Premium Oyster Sauce 2 tbsp

Seasoning Mix:
Sugar 90 g
White Vinegar 90 ml
Corn starch 1 tsp
Water 1 tbsp

Steps:
1)	Mix pork with marinade sauce, stir well.
2)	Heat 1 tsp oil and add ginger, bamboo shoots, carrots, fungus, and mushroom to pot. Cook and stir until done.
3)	Add Premium chicken Bouillon Powder, boiling water, pork and tofu to boil. Add all seasoning and stir well.
4)	Add sauce mix and stir well.

Japanese Fried Chicken (Karaage)

Ingredients
Chicken thigh (s) 250 g
Corn starch 1 /3 cup
Cake Flour 1 /3 cup
White sesame powder 1 /3 cup

Marinade
Premium Oyster Sauce 2 Tbsp 
Freshly Minced Garlic 1 tsp
Freshly Minced Ginger 1 tsp
Sake 11 /2 Tbsp

Seasoning Mix
Shichimi To Taste 
Directions
1.  Cut the chicken thigh into bite-sized pieces.
2.  Mix marinades well in a zipper bag, put #1 in and scrub thoroughly. Let them sit in the fridge for at least two hours.
3.  Make the coating: Mix one portion of cornstarch with one portion of cake flour. Add sesame in.
4.  Coat #2 evenly with #3.
5.  Deep-fry #4 in frying oil (medium heat) until it turns golden .brown.
6.  Shichimi sprinkle on top of chicken

Avocado Salsa with Tortilla Chips

Ingredients
Avocado, peeled, pitted 1 pc(90g)
Tomato, peeled and diced 120 g
Tortilla Chips 
Seasoning Mix
LIME JUICE 1 /2 tsp
Salt 
Vietnamese Style Lemon Dressing 1 1/2 Tbsp
Freshly Minced Garlic 1 tsp 
Directions
1.  Mash the avocado with fork
2.  Add tomato, lime juice, salt, black pepper powder, stir until well combined
3.  Add Vietnamese Style Lemon Dressing and blend until well combined
4.  Cover with plastic wrap
5.  Freeze in refrigerator for around 30 minutes
6.  Serve with Tortilla Chips

Hong Kong Style Faux Shark’s Fin Soup

Ingredients
Pork, shredded 200 g
Chicken breast, shredded 100 g
Bamboo shoots, shredded 75 g
Black fungus, soaked, shredded 75 g
Mushroom, soaked, shredded 75 g
Chinese vermicelli, soaked 120 g
Eggs 2 pcs
Water 2 L

Seasoning Mix
Premium Oyster Sauce 4 Tbsp
Soup Base for Chicken Hot Pot 2 Tbsp
Premium Soy Sauce 2 Tbsp
Premium Dark Soy Sauce 1 tsp
Pure Sesame Oil 2 Tbsp
Sugar 1 /2
Pepper powder to taste 
Water 3 Tbsp
5 Tbsp Cornflour 
Directions
1.  Soak Black fungus, mushrooms and Chinese vermicelli in water until soften.
2.  Shred chicken breast, bamboo shoots, soaked black fungus and mushrooms. 
3.  Pour over water, cover and bring to the boil over a high heat. Add chicken breast, mushroom, black fungus, bamboo shoots and Chinese vermicelli. Cook for 5 minutes or until done.
4.  Add seasonings and keep stirring until soup thicken. 
5.  Remove from the heat, stir in egg mixture.
6.  Add in beaten egg and stir after 5 seconds.

【Stewed Chicken with Kimchi and Chilli Bean Sauce】
Ingredients
Chicken 750 g
Kimchi 200 g
Freshly Minced Garlic 1 tbsp
Cabbage 50 g
Carrot (s) 50 g
Freshly Minced Ginger 1 tbsp
Leek 50 g

Marinade
Chili Bean Sauce 2 tbsp

Seasoning Mix
Premium Chicken Bouillon Powder 1 tsp
Corn starch 2 tsp
Sugar 1 tsp
Water 125 ml

Directions

2) Add chicken and stir-fry for about 2 minutes.

Spaghetti and Chicken in Char Siu Sauce with Mixed Herbs

Cucumber 120 g [sliced, or courgette]
Chicken meat 90 g [sliced]
Bell pepper (s) 1 /2pcs [red, sliced]
Water 550 ml

Marinade
Premium Oyster Sauce 1 tbsp

Seasoning Mix
Water 6 tbsp
Corn starch 1 tsp
Italian mixed herbs 1 tsp
Premium Soy Sauce 2 tsp
Char Siu Sauce 2 tsp
Premium Oyster Sauce 1 tsp

Directions
1) Cook spaghetti in boiling water according to packet instructions or until tender. Drain.
2) Mix chicken meat with marinade.

【Fried Fish in Double Deluxe Fruity Sauce】
Ingredients:
Fish fillet 350 g
Strawberry (ies) 2 pcs
Green grapes 2 pcs
Canned peach 25 g
Corn starch 
Marinade
Double Deluxe Soy Sauce 2 tsp

Seasoning Mix
Egg white 1 tbsp
Sugar 1 /4tsp
White pepper to taste 
Double Deluxe Soy Sauce 1 1/2tbsp
Grape juice 7 tbsp
Sugar 2 tsp

Directions

【Rice Dumplings with Golden Oysters and Dried Scallops】
Make this upcoming Dragon Boat Festival one to remember by getting busy in the kitchen...and let the wafting aromas of home-made steamed rice dumplings, brimming with juicy golden oysters and dried scallops usher in the festive season

Ingredients
semi-dried oysters (cooked) 4
dried scallops (cooked) 20 g
pork belly (cut into pieces) 40 g 
salted egg yolks 2
glutinous rice 90 g
peeled green beans 90 g
dried bamboo leaves 4 –8 
wrapping straws 4
Premium Chicken Bouillon Powder 1 Tbsp. 
Marinade
Five-spice powder 30 g 
Dipping Sauce
Sweet Soy Sauce 
Directions
1.  Soak the glutinous rice and green beans separately for 6 hours. Soak dumpling leaves and wrapping straws in water for 2 hours. Boil for 10 minutes before use. Drain well and set aside. 
2.  Marinate the pork belly overnight. Add half of the Lee Kum Kee Chicken Bouillon Powder into the glutinous rice, and the other half into the green beans. Mix well.
3.  Divide ingredients into two equal portions. Take one portion of each ingredient. Stack two bamboo leaves together and twist to form a funnel shape. Add a layer each of glutinous rice and green beans, then add pork belly, 2 golden oysters, dried scallop, 1 salted egg yolk, and the remaining green beans. Top off with rice.
4.  Fold the bamboo leaves to completely enclose the filling and form a pyramid shape. Tie securely with the straw. Repeat each step for the other dumpling.
5.  Bring water to boil in a large stock pot. Add the dumplings (must be completely covered by water). Simmer on low heat for 3 hours. Take off the heat. Keep covered for another 3 hours. Then serve.

[Fried Fish in Double Deluxe Fruity Sauce]

Egg white 1 tbsp
Sugar 1/4 tsp
White pepper to taste

Sauce mix

Grape juice 7 tbsp

1. Prepare marinade sauce.
